title: The Best Background Check Services for Small Businesses

interlinking_suggestions.title: What to Do If You've Been Misclassified as an Independent Contractor
A Guide to Background Checks for Employees

author.name: Elena Petrova
author.bio: An international business lawyer by trade, Elena breaks down the complexities of freelance contracts, corporate structures, and international liability. Her goal is to empower freelancers with the legal knowledge to operate confidently.

reviewedBy.name: Priya Singh, Esq.
reviewedBy.bio: Priya is an attorney specializing in international contract law for independent contractors. She ensures that the legal advice provided is accurate, actionable, and up-to-date with current regulations.
credentials: Member of the New York State Bar

key_takeaways: Calibrate your due diligence to the specific risk of each engagement, using informal checks for low-stakes projects and formal screenings for high-risk partners.
Before committing to a high-stakes contract, use a small, paid project as the ultimate real-world test of a contractor's skills, communication, and reliability.
For any US-based contractor screening, you must use an FCRA-compliant service and obtain written consent to avoid significant legal liability.
Recognize that US compliance laws do not apply internationally; engage a global screening specialist to navigate foreign regulations like GDPR when vetting partners abroad.
summary: Vetting contractors presents a significant challenge for small businesses, who must navigate legal complexities and costs to mitigate collaboration risks. The core advice is to adopt a tiered, risk-based framework: begin with foundational diligence like paid trial projects for low-risk roles, escalate to FCRA-compliant background checks for key domestic partners, and invest in specialist global screening for high-stakes engagements. Following this structured approach allows you to confidently protect your assets and reputation by matching the depth of your vetting to the level of risk, transforming compliance anxiety into strategic assurance.
ai_overview_answer: To properly vet contractors and freelancers, you should use a tiered, risk-based approach that matches the diligence to the level of risk. For low-stakes collaborations, start with foundational due diligence by conducting strategic reference checks, assessing their professional digital footprint, and using a small paid project to test their real-world performance. When a US-based contractor will access sensitive data, you must conduct a formal background check using a pay-as-you-go, FCRA-compliant service like GoodHire or Checkr. This process legally requires obtaining the contractor's explicit written consent before the screening. If you make a negative decision based on the report, you must follow the formal "adverse action" notification process. For high-risk international partners with access to finances or intellectual property, it is critical to use a specialist global screening service. These providers are equipped to navigate complex international data privacy laws, such as Europe's GDPR, ensuring the process is legally compliant in the contractor's jurisdiction. This structured method protects your business by ensuring your vetting is always appropriate for the engagement.

itemListElement.name: The Best Background Check Services for Small Businesses

@graph.headline: The Best Background Check Services for Small Businesses
@graph.description: Find the best background check services for any hire. Our guide covers everything from simple diligence to FCRA-compliant and global screening for contractors. Protect your business.

step.name: Tier 1: Is a Formal Background Check Even Necessary? Start with Foundational Due Diligence.

itemListElement.name: Go Beyond the Portfolio
itemListElement.text: A slick portfolio proves design skill, not professional competence. Investigate the process behind the product. Ask for detailed case studies or use an interview to probe for their specific contribution, the challenges they faced, and the measurable business outcomes they generated. This separates a true expert who drove results from someone who was merely part of a team that succeeded.

itemListElement.name: Conduct Strategic Reference Checks
itemListElement.text: Never rely solely on the hand-picked references a contractor provides. That list is curated for maximum positive effect. Instead, use a platform like LinkedIn to identify former managers or senior colleagues who worked with them on specific projects. A brief, professional message asking about their experience collaborating with the individual can yield far more candid and valuable insights into their reliability and communication patterns.

itemListElement.name: Assess Their Professional Digital Footprint
itemListElement.text: A professional's digital presence tells a story. Move beyond their curated website and analyze their LinkedIn activity, contributions to industry forums (like GitHub for developers or Behance for designers), or published articles. Are they positioned as a knowledgeable peer? Are their public interactions thoughtful and professional? This provides a powerful signal of their expertise and community standing.

itemListElement.name: The "Small Paid Project" Test
itemListElement.text: For a key contractor, the ultimate test is a real-world audition. Propose a small, well-defined, and paid initial project. This isn't a request for free work; it's a professional audition that evaluates their actual performance before you commit to a high-stakes engagement. This trial provides the best possible data on their communication style, adherence to deadlines, and quality of work, allowing you to make your final decision with confidence.

step.name: Tier 2: How to Vet a Key US-Based Contractor the Right Way

itemListElement.name: The FCRA Mandate is Non-Negotiable
itemListElement.text: The moment you use a third-party service to screen a potential contractor for business purposes, you enter a regulated space. The Fair Credit Reporting Act (FCRA) is the federal law governing this process, and it is your most important shield. While its application to independent contractors has been debated in some courts, the Federal Trade Commission (FTC) has consistently advised that the FCRA's protections should apply. The risk of non-compliance far outweighs the effort to comply. Using a non-compliant "people search" website for an employment screening is illegal and exposes your business to significant liability. Your first and most critical step is to only use an FCRA-compliant service.

itemListElement.name: Prioritize Pay-As-You-Go Services
itemListElement.text: The best background check services for a Business-of-One offer flexibility. Most major providers are built for corporate clients with high-volume needs and monthly subscriptions. You need a different model. Focus on reputable, FCRA-compliant providers like GoodHire or Checkr that offer "pay-as-you-go" or single-check packages. This approach prevents you from paying for unnecessary overhead while giving you access to the same powerful, legally sound tools the large companies use.

itemListElement.name: Master the Consent Process
itemListElement.text: You cannot legally run a background check without the contractor's explicit, written permission. A professional, FCRA-compliant platform is designed to manage this entire workflow for you. It will send a secure, electronic request to the contractor, provide the legally required disclosures, and collect their consent in a documented, defensible manner. This process protects both you and the contractor, ensuring transparency and legality from the start.

itemListElement.name: Understand "Adverse Action"
itemListElement.text: What if the report contains information that makes you decide against working with the contractor? This is a critical juncture where businesses can face significant legal exposure. If you make a negative decision based—in whole or in part—on the contents of a background check, you are legally required to follow the adverse action process. This is a formal, two-step procedure: 1. Pre-Adverse Action Notice: You must first send the candidate a notice that you are considering taking adverse action. This notice must include a copy of their background report and the document "A Summary of Your Rights Under the FCRA." 2. Waiting Period & Final Notice: You must give the candidate a reasonable amount of time (typically five business days) to review the report and dispute any inaccuracies with the screening company. After that period, if you finalize your decision, you must send a second, final adverse action notice. Reputable services have built-in compliance tools to help automate these required communications. Leaning on their expertise turns a daunting legal requirement into a clear, manageable workflow, allowing you to make difficult decisions fairly and legally.

step.name: Tier 3: How to Protect Your Assets When Vetting a Global Partner

itemListElement.name: Think Globally: FCRA is American, the World Has Other Rules
itemListElement.text: The first mental shift is acknowledging that compliance is not one-size-fits-all. The FCRA is a US law; its mandates end at the border. When you vet a contractor in the European Union, you enter an entirely different regulatory landscape governed by the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), which places even stricter rules on data privacy and consent. Recognizing that each country has its own legal framework is the first step toward a sound global strategy.

itemListElement.name: Choose Services with Verifiable International Capabilities
itemListElement.text: Many domestic screening services are not equipped for the complexities of a global search. You must actively vet a provider with demonstrated international expertise. Look beyond marketing claims and ask hard questions about their process for legally accessing and reporting on international criminal, educational, and employment records. Top-tier providers in this space, like HireRight or Veriff, have established networks and legal frameworks to perform this work correctly. You aren't just vetting the contractor; you are vetting your vetting partner.

itemListElement.name: Consent is King, but the Kingdom Changes
itemListElement.text: While the principle of consent remains paramount, its execution changes dramatically across borders. Obtaining permission from an international contractor requires a higher level of transparency and specificity. Under regulations like GDPR, the consent request must be explicit, clearly state what data you are collecting and for what specific purpose, and comply with local data privacy laws. A reputable global screening service will manage this complexity by providing jurisdiction-specific consent forms.

itemListElement.name: Frame the Cost as Insurance
itemListElement.text: A thorough international background check is a significant investment, costing substantially more than a domestic search. Reframe this not as an expense, but as an essential insurance policy. As cross-border legal analyst Mark Hunting advises, “Effective due diligence or screening to understand partners, employees, suppliers and service providers can help minimise legal, compliance and political risk.” You are not merely "buying a report"; you are buying protection against devastating outcomes.

mainEntity.name: Can I run a background check on a 1099 contractor or freelancer?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes. For roles with access to sensitive information, you should. The Federal Trade Commission (FTC) has clarified that the protections of the Fair Credit Reporting Act (FCRA) apply to independent contractors just as they do to traditional employees. This means you must use an FCRA-compliant service and obtain the contractor's written consent.

mainEntity.name: What is the best background check service for a single use?

acceptedAnswer.text: For a Business-of-One, avoiding hefty subscriptions is key. Look for reputable providers that explicitly offer **"pay-as-you-go"** or "no minimum" packages. Services like **GoodHire** and **Checkr** are well-regarded options that provide pricing tiers suitable for small businesses, allowing you to conduct a compliant check without a long-term commitment.

mainEntity.name: How do I legally vet an international contractor?

acceptedAnswer.text:
This process demands extreme care and a specialist provider.
1.  **Use a Global Specialist:** Engage a screening service with proven international capabilities to navigate local laws and access records correctly.
2.  **Comply with Local Laws:** Consent must be compliant with the contractor's local data privacy laws, such as the GDPR in Europe. A top-tier global provider will supply jurisdiction-specific consent forms.
3.  **Be Transparent:** Clearly communicate what you are checking and why to build trust and ensure a fair process.

mainEntity.name: Is a non-FCRA background check (e.g., from a people-search site) ever useful for business?

acceptedAnswer.text: This is a critical legal distinction. While you might use a people-search site in your *informal* initial diligence to verify a public footprint (like a past business address), you can **never** use information from a non-FCRA report to make a formal decision about whether to engage someone for work. The Consumer Financial Protection Bureau is clear: if a third-party report is used for employment decisions, it is a "consumer report," and both you and the provider must follow FCRA rules. Using a non-compliant service for this purpose exposes your business to serious legal risk.

mainEntity.name: What kind of information is in a professional background check?

acceptedAnswer.text:
The contents depend on the package you select, but a standard check typically includes:
*   **Identity Verification:** Confirms the individual is who they say they are using their Social Security Number.
*   **Criminal Records Search:** This includes a national criminal database search and often a check of sex offender registries.
*   **Comprehensive Options:** You can add searches for motor vehicle records, education verification, and employment history verification to confirm their stated credentials and experience.

mainEntity.name: How long does a background check take?

acceptedAnswer.text: Turnaround times vary. Many domestic criminal database searches return results within minutes or hours. However, checks that require manual verification—such as contacting universities or previous employers—can take a few business days. International checks will almost always take longer, often a week or more, due to the complexities of different legal systems and time zones.

body.content: Tier 1: Is a Formal Background Check Even Necessary? Start with Foundational Due Diligence.

A risk-based framework begins with a crucial question: do you even need a formal, paid screening? For many low-to-medium-stakes collaborations, the answer is no. Before you spend a dollar or navigate the complexities of compliance law, you can mitigate significant risk through smart, professional due diligence. This tier is your first line of defense, building a culture of careful vetting into your operations. Think of these steps not as hurdles, but as the essential groundwork for any secure and successful partnership.

items: **Go Beyond the Portfolio:** A slick portfolio proves design skill, not professional competence. Investigate the process behind the product. Ask for detailed case studies or use an interview to probe for their specific contribution, the challenges they faced, and the measurable business outcomes they generated. This separates a true expert who drove results from someone who was merely part of a team that succeeded.

**Conduct Strategic Reference Checks:** Never rely solely on the hand-picked references a contractor provides. That list is curated for maximum positive effect. Instead, use a platform like LinkedIn to identify former managers or senior colleagues who worked with them on specific projects. A brief, professional message asking about their experience collaborating with the individual can yield far more candid and valuable insights into their reliability and communication patterns.

**Assess Their Professional Digital Footprint:** A professional's digital presence tells a story. Move beyond their curated website and analyze their LinkedIn activity, contributions to industry forums (like GitHub for developers or Behance for designers), or published articles. Are they positioned as a knowledgeable peer? Are their public interactions thoughtful and professional? This provides a powerful signal of their expertise and community standing.

**The "Small Paid Project" Test:** For a key contractor, the ultimate test is a real-world audition. Propose a small, well-defined, and paid initial project. This isn't a request for free work; it's a professional audition that evaluates their actual performance before you commit to a high-stakes engagement. This trial provides the best possible data on their communication style, adherence to deadlines, and quality of work, allowing you to make your final decision with confidence.

body.content: Tier 2: How to Vet a Key US-Based Contractor the Right Way

The small paid project provides invaluable real-world data, but when a contractor will have deep access to sensitive client information, financial accounts, or your core intellectual property, you must escalate your diligence. This is where you move from informal vetting to a formal, legally compliant **background check**. As a solo professional, you cannot approach this like a massive corporation; you must do it correctly to avoid serious legal risk and transform compliance anxiety into confident control.

items: **The FCRA Mandate is Non-Negotiable:** The moment you use a third-party service to screen a potential contractor for business purposes, you enter a regulated space. The **Fair Credit Reporting Act (FCRA)** is the federal law governing this process, and it is your most important shield. While its application to independent contractors has been debated in some courts, the Federal Trade Commission (FTC) has consistently advised that the FCRA's protections should apply. The risk of non-compliance far outweighs the effort to comply. Using a non-compliant "people search" website for an employment screening is illegal and exposes your business to significant liability. Your first and most critical step is to *only* use an FCRA-compliant service.

**Prioritize Pay-As-You-Go Services:** The best background check services for a Business-of-One offer flexibility. Most major providers are built for corporate clients with high-volume needs and monthly subscriptions. You need a different model. Focus on reputable, FCRA-compliant providers like **GoodHire** or **Checkr** that offer "pay-as-you-go" or single-check packages. This approach prevents you from paying for unnecessary overhead while giving you access to the same powerful, legally sound tools the large companies use.

**Master the Consent Process:** You cannot legally run a background check without the contractor's explicit, written permission. A professional, FCRA-compliant platform is designed to manage this entire workflow for you. It will send a secure, electronic request to the contractor, provide the legally required disclosures, and collect their consent in a documented, defensible manner. This process protects both you and the contractor, ensuring transparency and legality from the start.

**Understand "Adverse Action":** What if the report contains information that makes you decide against working with the contractor? This is a critical juncture where businesses can face significant legal exposure. If you make a negative decision based—in whole or in part—on the contents of a background check, you are legally required to follow the **adverse action process**. This is a formal, two-step procedure:
    1.  **Pre-Adverse Action Notice:** You must first send the candidate a notice that you are *considering* taking adverse action. This notice must include a copy of their background report and the document "A Summary of Your Rights Under the FCRA."
    2.  **Waiting Period & Final Notice:** You must give the candidate a reasonable amount of time (typically five business days) to review the report and dispute any inaccuracies with the screening company. After that period, if you finalize your decision, you must send a second, final adverse action notice.

Reputable services have built-in compliance tools to help automate these required communications. Leaning on their expertise turns a daunting legal requirement into a clear, manageable workflow, allowing you to make difficult decisions fairly and legally.

body.content: Tier 3: How to Protect Your Assets When Vetting a Global Partner

A domestic, FCRA-compliant service provides a powerful shield, but that shield only protects you on home turf. For your most critical engagements—a business partner with access to your finances, or an international contractor handling your core IP—your due diligence must become comprehensive and global. This is not an operational expense; it is a direct investment in mitigating catastrophic risk.

items: **Think Globally: FCRA is American, the World Has Other Rules:** The first mental shift is acknowledging that compliance is not one-size-fits-all. The FCRA is a US law; its mandates end at the border. When you vet a contractor in the European Union, you enter an entirely different regulatory landscape governed by the **General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR)**, which places even stricter rules on data privacy and consent. Recognizing that each country has its own legal framework is the first step toward a sound global strategy.

**Choose Services with Verifiable International Capabilities:** Many domestic screening services are not equipped for the complexities of a global search. You must actively vet a provider with demonstrated international expertise. Look beyond marketing claims and ask hard questions about their process for legally accessing and reporting on international criminal, educational, and employment records. Top-tier providers in this space, like **HireRight** or **Veriff**, have established networks and legal frameworks to perform this work correctly. You aren't just vetting the contractor; you are vetting your vetting partner.

**Consent is King, but the Kingdom Changes:** While the principle of consent remains paramount, its execution changes dramatically across borders. Obtaining permission from an international contractor requires a higher level of transparency and specificity. Under regulations like GDPR, the consent request must be explicit, clearly state what data you are collecting and for what specific purpose, and comply with local data privacy laws. A reputable global screening service will manage this complexity by providing jurisdiction-specific consent forms.

**Frame the Cost as Insurance:** A thorough international background check is a significant investment, costing substantially more than a domestic search. Reframe this not as an expense, but as an essential insurance policy. As cross-border legal analyst Mark Hunting advises, “Effective due diligence or screening to understand partners, employees, suppliers and service providers can help minimise legal, compliance and political risk.” You are not merely "buying a report"; you are buying protection against devastating outcomes.

rows: Verifies a history of integrity and professionalism.
Uncovers criminal history or financial red flags.
Ensures you are not partnering with individuals involved in unethical practices.
Demonstrates a good-faith effort to comply with global anti-corruption and labor laws.

body.content: Investing in due diligence is a strategic decision that naturally leads to practical questions. As a global professional, you need direct, clear answers to operate confidently.

items:
**Can I run a background check on a 1099 contractor or freelancer?**
    Yes. For roles with access to sensitive information, you should. The Federal Trade Commission (FTC) has clarified that the protections of the Fair Credit Reporting Act (FCRA) apply to independent contractors just as they do to traditional employees. This means you must use an FCRA-compliant service and obtain the contractor's written consent.

**What is the best background check service for a single use?**
    For a Business-of-One, avoiding hefty subscriptions is key. Look for reputable providers that explicitly offer **"pay-as-you-go"** or "no minimum" packages. Services like **GoodHire** and **Checkr** are well-regarded options that provide pricing tiers suitable for small businesses, allowing you to conduct a compliant check without a long-term commitment.

**How do I legally vet an international contractor?**
    This process demands extreme care and a specialist provider.
    1.  **Use a Global Specialist:** Engage a screening service with proven international capabilities to navigate local laws and access records correctly.
    2.  **Comply with Local Laws:** Consent must be compliant with the contractor's local data privacy laws, such as the GDPR in Europe. A top-tier global provider will supply jurisdiction-specific consent forms.
    3.  **Be Transparent:** Clearly communicate what you are checking and why to build trust and ensure a fair process.

**Is a non-FCRA background check (e.g., from a people-search site) ever useful for business?**
    This is a critical legal distinction. While you might use a people-search site in your *informal* initial diligence to verify a public footprint (like a past business address), you can **never** use information from a non-FCRA report to make a formal decision about whether to engage someone for work. The Consumer Financial Protection Bureau is clear: if a third-party report is used for employment decisions, it is a "consumer report," and both you and the provider must follow FCRA rules. Using a non-compliant service for this purpose exposes your business to serious legal risk.

**What kind of information is in a professional background check?**
    The contents depend on the package you select, but a standard check typically includes:
    *   **Identity Verification:** Confirms the individual is who they say they are using their Social Security Number.
    *   **Criminal Records Search:** This includes a national criminal database search and often a check of sex offender registries.
    *   **Comprehensive Options:** You can add searches for motor vehicle records, education verification, and employment history verification to confirm their stated credentials and experience.

**How long does a background check take?**
    Turnaround times vary. Many domestic criminal database searches return results within minutes or hours. However, checks that require manual verification—such as contacting universities or previous employers—can take a few business days. International checks will almost always take longer, often a week or more, due to the complexities of different legal systems and time zones.

body.content: From Anxiety to Assurance: Your Due Diligence Action Plan

Navigating the nuances of consent, compliance, and global regulations is essential, but transforming that knowledge into a repeatable process is what truly protects your business. The sheer volume of concerns can make it tempting to either overspend on unnecessary checks or, worse, avoid them altogether. This is a false choice. The solution is not a generic checklist but a strategic, risk-based framework that gives you control. By adopting a tiered approach, you tailor your diligence to the specific risks of each engagement, ensuring you are always protected without being wasteful.
Your action plan is built on a simple principle: **the depth of your diligence must match the depth of the risk.**

rows: Low Risk: For collaborators with no access to sensitive data, financial systems, or core IP (e.g., a graphic designer for a one-off project).

Foundational Due Diligence. Focus on verifying their professional footprint. Conduct strategic reference checks, analyze their digital presence, and use the "small paid project" test as your ultimate tool.
Medium Risk: For key US-based contractors with access to sensitive client information, internal systems, or a mission-critical project role.
FCRA-Compliant Domestic Screening. Escalate to a formal background check using a reputable, pay-as-you-go service like GoodHire or Checkr to handle consent and ensure legal compliance.
High Risk: For potential business partners, international contractors handling core IP, or any individual with access to your financial accounts.

Comprehensive Global Assurance. Treat this as an investment, not an expense. Engage a specialist screening service with proven international capabilities to navigate complex laws like GDPR and ensure global compliance.

body.content: This structured approach transforms due diligence from a daunting legal hurdle into a core business strategy. It gives you a clear, scalable method for protecting your revenue, your reputation, and your intellectual property. By starting with foundational diligence, escalating to compliant domestic checks when necessary, and investing in comprehensive global assurance for your highest-stakes relationships, you build a resilient Business-of-One. This is how you stop reacting to risk and start growing with confidence.
